1: ṣārê yəhûḏāh ûbinyāmîn — 'adversaries of Judah and Benjamin.' The noun ṣar (H6862) means adversary, foe, oppressor; it is a common noun here, not a proper name. The same root underlies ṣārāh (distress) and stands opposite šālôm. The adversaries 'heard' (šāmaʿū) — the verb carries narrative weight throughout Ezra-Nehemiah; opposition is consistently triggered by news of building (cf. Nehemiah 2:10; Ezra 4:1).
2: nidroš — 'we worship' (Aramaic form of dāraš, H1875, lit. 'seek'). The claim is theologically loaded: to 'seek' God is covenant faithfulness language (cf. 2 Chronicles 14:4; Ezra 15:2). Their claim of shared worship is challenged by the context (2 Kings 17:24–41 describes syncretic practice among the transplanted peoples of Samaria). The phrase 'just as you do' (kākem) asserts equivalence the returning community rejects.
3: ên-lākem wālānû — 'you have no portion (ḥēleq) with us.' ḥēleq (H2506) is covenant-allocation language — the same word used for tribal land allotments (Joshua 18:5) and for Israel's share in YHWH (Psalms 16:5). Building the temple is a covenantal act belonging to those who stand in the covenant. Zerubbabel's refusal is not ethnic exclusion but a theological boundary: the mandate came from Cyrus to 'the people of Israel' specifically (Ezra 1:3).
4: mərapîm yəḏê ʿam-yəhûḏāh — 'weakening the hands of the people of Judah.' The hiphil participle of rāpāh (H7503) governs the sentence: lit. 'causing the hands to be slack.' 'Weak hands' is a Hebrew idiom for discouragement and inability to act (Isaiah 13:7; Jeremiah 38:4; Ezekiel 7:17; Nehemiah 6:9). The idiom recurs in Nehemiah 6:9 when Nehemiah's enemies try the same tactic. ûmévahălîm ôtām — hiphil of bāhal (H926): 'unsettling, alarming them' — the pairing of external discouragement and internal alarm is the two-pronged opposition strategy throughout the book.
5: yōʿăṣîm — 'counselors, advisors' (H3289 yāʿaṣ, qal ptcp). Hired consultants to frustrate (pārar, H6565, 'break, frustrate') the Judahites' plans in the courts of Cyrus and Darius. The funding of legal interference (Aramaic ‎apar in the later letters) is the escalation from discouragement (v4) to institutional opposition.
6: This verse marks a temporal jump to the reign of Ahasuerus (Xerxes, 486–465 BCE), decades after Cyrus. The Chronicler (author of Ezra) uses this anachronistic grouping to show the pattern of opposition across multiple Persian reigns rather than strict chronological sequence. The accusation is a śiṭnāh (H7855) — formal complaint; the word is cognate with śāṭan (accuser) and evokes the legal-accusatory role. Opposition to the covenant community takes the shape of legal slander before the king.
7: Last Hebrew verse before the Aramaic block begins. Three names (Bishlam, Mithredath, Tabeel) write to Artaxerxes. biktāb ʾărāmît — 'in Aramaic script (kətāb) and translated into Aramaic (məturgam ʾărāmît).' The phrase signals the transition: the next section (Ezra 4:8–Ezra 6:18) is written in Aramaic and preserves actual imperial correspondence in the chancellery language of the Persian court. This is a remarkable feature of the Hebrew canon — nearly two chapters of Scripture are composed in Aramaic, not Hebrew.
8: Aramaic begins here (Ezra 4:8–Ezra 6:18). Rehum baʿēl ṭəʿēm — 'Rehum the commander' (lit. 'master of decree/report'; H2942 ṭəʿēm = taste/judgment/report, an Aramaic official title). Shimshai is sāpərāʾ — the scribe (Aramaic form of Hebrew sōpēr). These two frame the letter as a legal document with proper credentialing. The letter is composed (kātab) as official imperial correspondence — the Aramaic verb and vocabulary throughout mark it as authentic chancellery style.
9: The enumeration of peoples in the greeting formula follows Aramaic epistolary convention. dināyēʾ, ʾăpārsātəkāyēʾ etc. — various ethnic groups settled in Samaria by Osnappar (= Ashurbanipal, Assyrian king, 668–627 BCE). The list witnesses to the Assyrian deportation and resettlement policy (2 Kings 17:24). The ethnic diversity of the signatories strengthens the legal claim by multiplying the number of aggrieved parties.
10: ʾOsnapper (Ashurbanipal) is identified as 'great and noble' (rab wəyaqqîr) — the letter uses imperial honorifics for the previous Assyrian king even within a petition to the Persian king, flattering the tradition of powerful empire. Trans-Euphrates (ʿăḇar naharāh, lit. 'beyond the river') is the standard Persian administrative designation for the western satrapy including Syria-Palestine.
11: The letter's formal heading: ʿaḇdāk — 'your servants' — the standard submissive epistolary opener. The phrase 'men of Trans-Euphrates' frames the letter as coming from loyal subjects reporting a threat to the king's interests. Aramaic administrative letters from Elephantine (5th century BCE) follow the same 'your servants' convention, corroborating the authentic epistolary style of this section.
12: mārādā' wəbîšəttāʾ — 'rebellious and evil' (Aramaic mrd = to rebel, H4779). The letter characterizes Jerusalem twice with rebellion vocabulary before describing the building. šûrāyāʾ yəšaklilûn — 'they are finishing the walls' (Aramaic šāklal, H7955, to complete). The present-tense framing ('they are rebuilding … they are completing') makes the threat immediate rather than historical.
13: mindāh bəlô wăhălāk — three tax terms: mindāh (tribute, H4061), bəlô (toll, H1093), hălāk (customs/duty, H1983). All three are Persian taxation categories; their enumeration in Aramaic mirrors actual Achaemenid administrative vocabulary known from other sources. The legal argument is purely economic: rebuilding Jerusalem = tax revenue loss. The letter weaponizes fiscal concerns.
14: melaḥ hêkəlāʾ məliḥnāʾ — 'we eat the salt of the palace' (lit. 'we are salted with the salt of the palace'). Eating salt with someone creates a covenant of loyalty (cf. Ezra 6:9 where salt is part of offerings; Numbers 18:19 'covenant of salt'). To eat the king's salt and then be silent about threats to his honor would be covenant betrayal. The idiom explains the moral urgency of their report — loyalty demands disclosure.
15: sēper dāḵrânāyāʾ — 'the record books' (Aramaic; dāḵrôn, H1799, memorial record/chronicle). The request to search the archives is historically realistic: Persian imperial archives were extensive and systematically organized. The claim that Jerusalem 'has long been rebellious' is a distortion of history framed as a search for precedent — using the empire's own administrative memory against the building project.
16: ḥălāq lāʾ yihweh lāk — 'you will have no portion (ḥălāq) left in Trans-Euphrates.' The same covenant-allocation word from v3 now appears in the adversaries' letter — but weaponized: if Jerusalem is rebuilt, the king's territorial claim collapses. The irony is precise: the community that was told 'you have no portion' in the building now uses 'portion' language as a threat. The letter mirrors the rejection of v3.
17: pəgam — 'reply/decree' (Aramaic, H6600; related to Persian paiγāma, message). The king's answer is formally sent back: šəlām wəkaʿet — 'peace, and now' is the standard Aramaic transition between greeting and substance in imperial correspondence. Artaxerxes' reply follows the exact counter-letter convention.
18: pəraš miqrēʾ qoḏāmay — 'read aloud (qərāʾ) in full before me' (lit. 'translated and read before me'). pəraš (H6568, Aramaic; = Hebrew pāraš) means to make distinct/translate — suggesting the letter may have been translated from Aramaic into Persian for the king's personal reading. The king's response demonstrates he received and processed the full text, legitimizing the subsequent decree.
19: šəliṭîn malkîn hāwôw bîrûšəlem — 'powerful kings have ruled in Jerusalem' — the archive search confirms the accusation. The Aramaic šəliṭ (H7990) = one having authority/power. The historical claim is accurate (David and Solomon's empires did extend to Trans-Euphrates), which makes the legal precedent usable. The search confirms the letter's accusation by its own logic.
20: minkās, bəlô wǎhăḵāk — the same three-tax formula from v13 reappears in the king's reply, confirming he understood and accepted the fiscal framing. The parallel wording between the accusatory letter and the royal decree shows the accusers have successfully set the terms of the discussion: Jerusalem = fiscal threat = must be stopped.
21: śîmû ṭəʿēm — 'issue a command' (lit. 'place a decree'). ṭəʿēm (H2942) recurs as the key word for royal decrees throughout Ezra (cf. Ezra 4:8; Ezra 5:3,9; Ezra 6:1,3,8,11,12; Ezra 7:13,21,23). The provisional character of the stop order is built into the text: 'until I give a further order' (minněnî ṭəʿēm yitnəśēm) — the suspension is temporary, leaving open the door for Darius's reversal in Ezra 6.
22: šālû lemeʿbaḏ pəšāʿ ḏənāh — 'be careful not to neglect this matter.' Aramaic šəlī (H7960) = negligence/error; pəšāʿ (H6588) = breach/transgression — the same root as Hebrew pāšaʿ (sin/rebellion). The rhetorical question 'Why should damage grow to hurt the king?' is a fiscal argument. The adversaries have successfully reframed a theological dispute (who may build the temple) as a purely political/economic threat.
23: bəhipparaʿ — 'as soon as' (lit. 'in the uncovering/reading of'). The speed of the officials' response (they 'rushed' or 'hurried,' ʾōḏaynāʾ) demonstrates the leverage of an imperial letter. baʾdraʿ wəḥayil — 'by force and power' — Aramaic for armed compulsion. The rhetorical sequence: letter → decree → immediate armed enforcement. The adversaries achieve total legal and military victory. Temporally this is the nadir of the building narrative.
24: bəṭēlat ʿăḇîḏat bêt-ʾĕlāhāʾ — 'the work of God's house in Jerusalem stopped.' Aramaic bəṭēl (H989) = to cease, be idle — the same root in the NT Greek katargeō (to make ineffective). ʿaḏ šənat tartên — 'until the second year of Darius.' The narrative resumes in Ezra 5. The temple stoppage lasted approximately 16 years (ca. 534–520 BCE). The closing reference to Darius creates the narrative bridge to the next movement — a reversal is anticipated.
